title: What Anxiety Is, Biologically
subtitle: A reading on what anxiety is in the body, why it is doing this, and what works to settle it
excerpt: A twenty-minute reading on what anxiety actually is in the body. The alarm system, the hormones, the breath. Not a flaw of character, not a moral failing. A nervous system doing its job, often louder than the day calls for.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The reading draws on the wider literature on the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems, on the work of Stephen Porges (Polyvagal Theory, 1994), and on Bessel van der Kolk's writing in The Body Keeps the Score (2014).

content.text: Anxiety is not a flaw of character. It is the body's alarm system doing what alarm systems do. The system is built to keep you safe. Sometimes it fires when there is no real threat. Sometimes it fires at the wrong volume. The system is still doing its job.
What is happening in the body

The brain reads the situation. If it picks up a threat, it sends a fast signal to the body. The signal turns on the sympathetic nervous system. The heart speeds up. The breath gets shallow. Blood moves to the big muscles. The body is ready to run, fight, or freeze.

Two hormones do most of the work. Adrenaline is the fast one. It hits in seconds. Cortisol is the slower one. It builds up over the next half hour and stays for hours. Together they keep the body ready for action.
Why the system fires when the threat is not real

The system does not know the difference between a tiger and a difficult email. The brain reads the body's state and reads the situation, and decides on the alarm. If the body is already in a high state, a small input can tip the whole system into alarm.

Modern life keeps the body in a higher baseline state than the system was designed for. Sleep is shorter. Caffeine is more. Screens add input all day. Real rest is harder to find. The alarm fires more often because the baseline never gets low.
Why the breath matters so much
The breath is the one part of the system you can change on purpose. The heart rate and the hormones are not direct. The breath is direct.

A long out-breath signals the parasympathetic nervous system to switch on. The parasympathetic is the brake. When the brake is on, the heart slows and the body settles. The fastest way to turn the brake on is to make the out-breath longer than the in-breath.
What works in a live moment
Long out-breath, six rounds. Six in. Eight or ten out.
Cold water on the wrists or the face. The body reads it as a small reset.
Feet on the floor. Eyes on five things in the room. Naming them one at a time.
Tapping. The eight points settle the body the same way the breath does.
What works over weeks
Sleep. The system runs hotter on too little sleep.
Less caffeine, or none after lunchtime.
Daily movement. Not workout-level. Walking is enough.
A daily practice that turns the brake on. Five minutes is enough. Pick a shape that works for your day.
Talking. Not pushing through. Anxiety is heavier carried alone.
Where this reading comes from
Original to homemade.education. The reading draws on the wider writing on the nervous system. See sourceNotes for the books and the researchers.
